relationshipToCourse: Exam-style practice source; not confirmed against Vinci's June 2024 manual
pilotNote: This is a 25-item pilot batch covering only the first 25 of 91 items in the source PDF, intended to validate the intake schema before processing the remaining 66. See docs/CONTENT_INTAKE_BENCHMARK.md for how this source's Tier 3 status affects what it can be used for. No item in this file may be marked verified — per the benchmark doc's rule, verification requires tracing to Vinci's actual materials or an official source, neither of which this file is.

items.originalQuestion: the term "pintle" and "gudgeon" are associated with the
items.originalAnswer: rudder
items.cleanedQuestion: The terms "pintle" and "gudgeon" are associated with which piece of lifeboat equipment?
items.cleanedAnswer: The rudder.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Clear vocabulary item; no ambiguity.

items.originalQuestion: When using the term limber system one is referring to a
items.originalAnswer: drainage system holes in foors allows water to fow thru compartments
items.cleanedQuestion: When using the term "limber system," what is being referred to?
items.cleanedAnswer: A drainage system: holes in the floors (limber holes) that let water flow through compartments toward the bilge/drain.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: No subtopic bullet named 'limber system' in the given manual TOC; mapped to the closest construction-related subtopic (Interior Construction). OCR errors 'foors'/'fow' corrected to 'floors'/'flow'.

items.originalQuestion: The standard magnetic compass heading differs from the true heading by
items.originalAnswer: compass error
items.cleanedQuestion: The standard magnetic compass heading differs from the true heading by what amount/factor?
items.cleanedAnswer: Compass error (the combined effect of variation and deviation).
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Exact duplicate of source item 37 (outside this 25-item pilot). Also closely related to variation and deviation items later in the source — good candidate for a compare/contrast question distinguishing compass error vs. variation vs. deviation once verified.

items.originalQuestion: After an engine is started you should
items.originalAnswer: check operating pressures and temperatures, and check for leaks
items.cleanedQuestion: After starting a lifeboat engine, what should you do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Check operating pressures and temperatures, and check for leaks.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Clear procedural item; no ambiguity.

items.originalQuestion: A certifed lifeboatman assigned to command the lifeboat must
items.originalAnswer: have a list of the persons assigned to the lfieboat
items.cleanedQuestion: A certified lifeboatman assigned to command a lifeboat must do what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Have a list of the persons assigned to that lifeboat.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typos 'certifed'/'lfieboat' corrected. Lifeboat-specific command duty could alternatively sit under Chapter 2's lifeboat-crew content instead of Chapter 1.

items.originalQuestion: The survival crafts engine is fueled with
items.originalAnswer: diesel oil
items.cleanedQuestion: The survival craft's engine is fueled with what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Diesel oil.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Near-duplicate of source item 44 (outside this 25-item pilot).

items.originalQuestion: When inspecting a survival craft, you should check to make sure
items.originalAnswer: hydraulic pressure is within specifed range
items.cleanedQuestion: When inspecting a survival craft, what should you check?
items.cleanedAnswer: That hydraulic pressure is within the specified range.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Not clear whether this refers to engine hydraulics, steering hydraulics, or a hydraulic davit/release system — any of the three is plausible. Mapped to Propulsion Systems as the best single guess.

items.originalQuestion: When a sea anchor is used in landing stern frst in a heavy surf, sternway is checked by
items.originalAnswer: slacking the tripping line and towing the sea anchor by holding the line
items.cleanedQuestion: When a sea anchor is used to land stern-first in heavy surf, how is sternway (backward motion) checked/controlled?
items.cleanedAnswer: By slacking the tripping line and towing the sea anchor, holding it by the line.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'frst' corrected to 'first'.

items.originalQuestion: Your vessel is equipped with totally enclosed lifeboats. Which is true when the boat is enveloped in fames?
items.originalAnswer: An air tank will provide about ten minutes of air for the survivors and the engine
items.cleanedQuestion: Your vessel is equipped with totally enclosed lifeboats. Which statement is true when the boat is enveloped in flames?
items.cleanedAnswer: An air tank provides about ten minutes of air for the survivors and the engine while passing through flames.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'fames' corrected to 'flames'. Good misconception-trap material: a student might assume a totally enclosed lifeboat offers indefinite fire protection, when the real answer specifies a firm ~10-minute air supply limit. Fire-protection/air-supply systems aren't an explicitly named subtopic in the given TOC excerpt; mapped to the closest equipment bucket.

items.originalQuestion: Most lifeboats are equipped with
items.originalAnswer: unbalanced rudders
items.cleanedQuestion: Most lifeboats are equipped with what type of rudder?
items.cleanedAnswer: Unbalanced rudders.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Clear vocabulary/fact item.

items.originalQuestion: Your vessel has lifeboats on both sides. Lifeboat #2 is located
items.originalAnswer: forwards of lifeboat #4 on the port side
items.cleanedQuestion: Your vessel has lifeboats on both sides, numbered per standard convention. Where is Lifeboat #2 located?
items.cleanedAnswer: Forward of Lifeboat #4, on the port side.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Standard even-numbers-port/odd-numbers-starboard, bow-to-stern numbering convention; should be double-checked against the manual's exact numbering rule before use.

items.originalQuestion: Stretchers are fitted in lifeboats to provide a
items.originalAnswer: place for rowers to brace their feet
items.cleanedQuestion: Stretchers are fitted in lifeboats to provide what?
items.cleanedAnswer: A place for rowers to brace their feet while rowing.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: RECONSTRUCTED: in the source PDF this item's number and answer appeared at the bottom of one page while its question text appeared unnumbered at the top of the next page (a page-break artifact of the original two-column layout). Reassembled here from both fragments.

items.originalQuestion: preventer bars are ftted on lifeboat releasing gear to prevent
items.originalAnswer: accidental unhooking when the falls become slacked
items.cleanedQuestion: Preventer bars are fitted on lifeboat releasing gear to prevent what?
items.cleanedAnswer: Accidental unhooking when the falls become slack.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'ftted' corrected to 'fitted'.

items.originalQuestion: On a lifeboat equipped with a Rottmer type releasing gear, turning the releasing lever
items.originalAnswer: both falls at the same time even if the boat has not reached the water
items.cleanedQuestion: On a lifeboat equipped with Rottmer-type releasing gear, what happens when the releasing lever is turned?
items.cleanedAnswer: It releases both falls at the same time, even if the boat has not yet reached the water.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Original answer text was a sentence fragment ('...both falls...'); reconstructed into a full sentence. Good misconception-trap material: a student might assume this gear only releases once the boat is safely waterborne, but the real danger is that it releases immediately regardless of the boat's position — a genuine safety hazard if triggered too early.

items.originalQuestion: Prior to lowering the lifeboat, the most important item to check is the
items.originalAnswer: boat plug
items.cleanedQuestion: Prior to lowering the lifeboat, what is the most important item to check?
items.cleanedAnswer: The boat (drain) plug.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Could also work as a misconception-trap (a student might guess fuel, oars, or equipment inventory as 'most important' before the far more basic drain plug), but framed here as a straightforward procedure item.

items.originalQuestion: When launching a lifeboat, frapping lines should be rigged
items.originalAnswer: at the embarkation deck
items.cleanedQuestion: When launching a lifeboat, where should frapping lines be rigged?
items.cleanedAnswer: At the embarkation deck.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: In launching a covered lifeboat what would the safely lower the lifeboat from the inside of the cabin
items.originalAnswer: winch remote control wire
items.cleanedQuestion: When launching a covered lifeboat, what allows it to be safely lowered from inside the cabin?
items.cleanedAnswer: A winch remote control wire.
items.mappedSubtopic: Launching a Lifeboat from a Gravity Davit
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Original question phrasing was slightly garbled ('what would the safely lower'); cleaned for grammar without changing meaning.

items.originalQuestion: What is the best procedures for picking up a lifeboat at sea while utilizing the lifeboats sea painter
items.originalAnswer: Place the lifeboat ahead and to the leeward of your ship with the wind about the broad don the bow of your ship
items.cleanedQuestion: What is the best procedure for picking up a lifeboat at sea using the lifeboat's sea painter?
items.cleanedAnswer: Place the lifeboat ahead of and to the leeward of your ship, with the wind about broad on the bow of your ship.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR error 'broad don the bow' corrected to 'broad on the bow' (standard bearing terminology).

items.originalQuestion: When landing a lifeboat through heavy surf with a strong current running parallel to the beach (from right to left when facing from seaward) what is the recommended procedure?
items.originalAnswer: Rig a drogue with a tripping line over the bow, back ashore with drogue tripped between breakers
items.cleanedQuestion: When landing a lifeboat through heavy surf with a strong current running parallel to the beach (right to left, facing seaward), what is the recommended procedure?
items.cleanedAnswer: Rig a drogue with a tripping line over the bow, then back toward shore, tripping the drogue between breakers.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None significant.

items.originalQuestion: You have abandoned ship and you fnd yourself aboard a lifeboat in a heavy sea. Your boat is able to make way through the water. To prevent broaching you should
items.originalAnswer: Head the boat into the swells to take them at a 30 to 40 degree angle on either a bow and run as slow as possible without losing steerage
items.cleanedQuestion: You have abandoned ship and find yourself aboard a lifeboat in a heavy sea, able to make way through the water. To prevent broaching, what should you do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Head the boat into the swells, taking them at a 30-40 degree angle on either bow, and run as slow as possible without losing steerage.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: RECONSTRUCTED: this question's text was split across a PDF page break (the first part and answer on one page, the continuation 'through the water. To prevent broaching you should' unnumbered at the top of the next page). Reassembled here. OCR typo 'fnd' corrected to 'find'.

items.originalQuestion: When backing a motor propelled lifeboat (right-hand propeller) with rudder amidships, the stern will back to
items.originalAnswer: to port
items.cleanedQuestion: When backing a motor-propelled lifeboat with a right-hand propeller and rudder amidships, which way will the stern back?
items.cleanedAnswer: To port.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: Good misconception-trap material: propeller-walk effects when backing are counterintuitive and commonly guessed wrong (students often assume the stern swings the same direction as ahead-propulsion steering would suggest).

items.originalQuestion: The painter which is to be attached tot the thwart of a lifeboat should
items.originalAnswer: have a long eye splice at the end and a hardwood toggle should be attached to the thwart with a lanyard
items.cleanedQuestion: A painter to be attached to the thwart of a lifeboat should have what features?
items.cleanedAnswer: A long eye splice at the end, with a hardwood toggle attached to the thwart by a lanyard.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: OCR typo 'tot' corrected to 'to'. Could alternatively fit under a launching-specific subtopic since painters are launch/retrieval gear.

items.originalQuestion: Lifeboats hatchets should be
items.originalAnswer: secured at each end of the boat with a lanyard
items.cleanedQuestion: How should lifeboat hatchets be stowed?
items.cleanedAnswer: Secured at each end of the boat with a lanyard.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: When using the lifeboat compass, you must be careful to
items.originalAnswer: Set it on the centerline of the boat, apply the correction for compass error, keep metal objects away from it
items.cleanedQuestion: When using the lifeboat compass, what must you be careful to do?
items.cleanedAnswer: Set it on the centerline of the boat, apply the correction for compass error, and keep metal objects away from it.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.

items.originalQuestion: The quantity of fuel required to be carried in a motor lifeboat is
items.originalAnswer: the quantity of fuel needed for 24hrs of continuous operation
items.cleanedQuestion: How much fuel is required to be carried in a motor lifeboat?
items.cleanedAnswer: Enough fuel for 24 hours of continuous operation.
items.notesOnAmbiguity: None.
